## Management Summary

### Verdict

🔴 Not production-ready. Every primary control boundary - authentication, query construction, server-side code execution, secret management, and output encoding - is broken or absent. An anonymous internet attacker can achieve administrative access, read the full user database, or execute arbitrary code on the server without chaining multiple vulnerabilities; each capability is independently available through a single entry point.

**Risk distribution:** 🔴 Critical: 11 · 🟠 High: 24 · 🟡 Medium: 3 · 🟢 Low: 0 · **Total: 38**

<br/>

**Worst-case scenarios behind this verdict - what an attacker could do today:**

<blockquote style="border-left: 3px solid #dc2626; background: #fef2f2; padding: 16px 20px; margin: 0;">

- **Full admin takeover using the public repository** — The RSA key used to sign every login session is committed to the source code repository; any reader can mint a session token with administrator privileges and access every protected admin route without supplying a valid credential. *([F-002](#f-002), [F-003](#f-003))*
- **Remote code execution via the B2B ordering endpoint** — The B2B order processor evaluates user-supplied order content as executable code inside a sandbox that can be escaped; a crafted order payload gives an attacker full server-side code execution as the application process. *([F-011](#f-011))*
- **Database dump via the login and search endpoints** — Both the login and product-search forms build their database queries by pasting user input directly into the SQL string; an attacker can read every table in the database — including all user credentials — without logging in. *([F-001](#f-001), [F-005](#f-005))*
- **Immediate offline cracking of every stolen password** — All user passwords are stored as short, unsalted hashes using an algorithm designed for speed rather than security; any credential stolen via database access, file leak, or key theft can be reversed to plaintext in seconds using freely available tools. *([F-007](#f-007), [F-009](#f-009))*
- **Stored malicious scripts delivered to all admin users** — Any attacker who can submit a product review or description can embed a script that executes in every administrator's browser because the admin panel renders stored content as raw HTML without sanitisation and no browser policy blocks inline script execution. *([F-037](#f-037), [F-035](#f-035))*

</blockquote>

<br/>

No single patch closes the exposure; the codebase requires parameterised queries, runtime-injected secrets, replacement of the eval-based order processor, and a Content-Security-Policy before any of the above scenarios can be closed.

**Attack-chain analysis.** 2 findings anchor 2 code-verified attack chains (see [§9](#9-abuse-cases) Abuse Cases): 🔴 [F-002](#f-002) — Hardcoded RSA private key enables JWT forgery, 🟠 [F-038](#f-038) — Client side only admin guard Angular route guard bypass app.guard.ts. Their Critical/High ratings reflect exploitability confirmed end-to-end across the chain, not the individual weakness alone.

### Security Posture & Top Threats

Grey edges are the legitimate request backbone. **Solid red** edges are attacker-controlled attacks - each originates at a threat actor and lands on the component it directly reaches. **Dotted red** edges are the consequence path: how that attack propagates through the application tier onto the data tier or the victim. Each red edge carries the threat glyph(s) - the same numbering as Figure 2 and the Top Threats table below.

**Threat actors.** The actors below drive the numbered attack paths in the figures above; the Shop User is the *victim* of client-side attacks (XSS / CSRF), not an attacker.

- **Shop User** — legitimate customer; target of client-side attacks; target of ⑦ CSRF / Permissive CORS.
- **Anonymous Internet Attacker** — no account; registers in seconds when needed; drives ① Insecure Query Construction & Data Access, ② Hardcoded Secrets & Weak Cryptography, ④ Sensitive File & Secret Exposure, ⑥ Output Encoding / Cross-Site Scripting.
- **Authenticated Internet Attacker** — owns a regular account; logged in; drives ③ Broken Authorization & Access Control, ⑤ Remote Code Execution (unsafe eval).

**7 structural threats**, grouped by weakness class - each row is one threat, not one finding. *Threat Description* states the general architectural weakness (STRIDE in brackets); *Findings* lists the concrete instances, each linked to [§8 Findings Register](#8-findings-register) with its component; *Risk & Impact* combines severity with business consequence.

| # | Threat Description | Findings (→ Component) | Risk & Impact | Fix |
|---|------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------|------------------------------------|--------|
| <a id="path-injection"></a>① | **Insecure Query Construction & Data Access** _(T·I)_<br/>User input flows into raw SQL or NoSQL queries without parameterisation, allowing authentication bypass or data exfiltration. | •&nbsp;🔴&nbsp;[F-001](#f-001) — SQL injection in login →&nbsp;[C-01](#c-01)<br/>•&nbsp;🔴&nbsp;[F-005](#f-005) — SQL injection in product search →&nbsp;[C-01](#c-01)<br/>•&nbsp;🔴&nbsp;[F-010](#f-010) — XXE via XML upload with external entity resolution →&nbsp;[C-04](#c-04)<br/>•&nbsp;🟠&nbsp;[F-017](#f-017) — NoSQL injection in review update no author verification →&nbsp;[C-01](#c-01)<br/>•&nbsp;🟠&nbsp;[F-025](#f-025) — NoSQL injection via \$where clause in product reviews →&nbsp;[C-01](#c-01) | 🔴 **Critical**<br/>Full Admin Takeover · Customer Data Exfiltration | ❶ [M-001](#m-001)<br/>❶ [M-002](#m-002) |
| <a id="path-auth-bypass"></a>② | **Hardcoded Secrets & Weak Cryptography** _(S·E)_<br/>Authentication can be forged or bypassed because JWT signing keys and admin credentials are committed in source and password hashes use unsalted `MD5`. | •&nbsp;🔴&nbsp;[F-002](#f-002) — Hardcoded RSA private key enables JWT forgery →&nbsp;[C-01](#c-01)<br/>•&nbsp;🔴&nbsp;[F-003](#f-003) — JWT algorithm none bypass via express jwt →&nbsp;[C-01](#c-01)<br/>•&nbsp;🔴&nbsp;[F-004](#f-004) — Hardcoded admin credentials in static user data →&nbsp;[C-03](#c-03)<br/>•&nbsp;🔴&nbsp;[F-007](#f-007) — MD5 password hashing trivially reversible →&nbsp;[C-01](#c-01)<br/>•&nbsp;🔴&nbsp;[F-009](#f-009) — User passwords stored as unsalted MD5 data at rest →&nbsp;[C-03](#c-03)<br/>•&nbsp;🟠&nbsp;[F-014](#f-014) — Hardcoded HMAC secret enables coupon forgery →&nbsp;[C-01](#c-01) | 🔴 **Critical**<br/>Full Admin Takeover · Customer Data Exfiltration | ❶ [M-003](#m-003)<br/>❶ [M-004](#m-004) |
| <a id="path-privilege-escalation"></a>③ | **Broken Authorization & Access Control** _(E·I)_<br/>Authorisation checks are absent server-side or enforced only in the Angular client, allowing any authenticated user to reach admin routes or modify other users' data. | •&nbsp;🟠&nbsp;[F-013](#f-013) — Unauthenticated product modification via PUT /api/Products/:id →&nbsp;[C-01](#c-01)<br/>•&nbsp;🟠&nbsp;[F-019](#f-019) — User enumeration via /api/Users →&nbsp;[C-01](#c-01)<br/>•&nbsp;🟠&nbsp;[F-033](#f-033) — B2B API reachable by any authenticated user →&nbsp;[C-01](#c-01)<br/>•&nbsp;🟠&nbsp;[F-038](#f-038) — Client side only admin guard Angular route guard bypass →&nbsp;[C-02](#c-02) | 🟠 **High**<br/>Full Admin Takeover · Customer Data Exfiltration | ❷ [M-011](#m-011)<br/>❷ [M-010](#m-010) |
| <a id="path-sensitive-data-exposure"></a>④ | **Sensitive File & Secret Exposure** _(I)_<br/>Confidential files, access logs, JWT signing keys, and the unencrypted SQLite database are reachable via unauthenticated HTTP endpoints or readable from the container filesystem. | •&nbsp;🔴&nbsp;[F-006](#f-006) — Confidential files exposed via /ftp directory listing →&nbsp;[C-01](#c-01)<br/>•&nbsp;🟠&nbsp;[F-020](#f-020) — Access logs exposed via /support/logs directory listing →&nbsp;[C-01](#c-01)<br/>•&nbsp;🟠&nbsp;[F-021](#f-021) — JWT public key and premium.key exposed at /encryptionkeys →&nbsp;[C-01](#c-01)<br/>•&nbsp;🟠&nbsp;[F-024](#f-024) — SQLite database file on writable filesystem without encryption data/juiceshop.s… →&nbsp;[C-03](#c-03)<br/>•&nbsp;🟠&nbsp;[F-026](#f-026) — SSRF via profile image URL upload →&nbsp;[C-04](#c-04)<br/>•&nbsp;🟠&nbsp;[F-036](#f-036) — Directory traversal in ZIP file upload →&nbsp;[C-04](#c-04)<br/>•&nbsp;🟡&nbsp;[F-039](#f-039) — Open redirect via /redirect?to= parameter →&nbsp;[C-01](#c-01)<br/>•&nbsp;🟡&nbsp;[F-041](#f-041) — Prometheus metrics exposed unauthenticated at /metrics →&nbsp;[C-01](#c-01) | 🔴 **Critical**<br/>Customer Data Exfiltration | ❶ [M-012](#m-012)<br/>❷ [M-014](#m-014) |
| <a id="path-remote-code-execution"></a>⑤ | **Remote Code Execution (unsafe eval)** _(E)_<br/>User-supplied content is executed server-side via an unsafe VM sandbox escape in the B2B order processor and via a template-injection sink in the user-profile route. | •&nbsp;🔴&nbsp;[F-011](#f-011) — RCE via vm.runInContext sandbox escape in B2B order →&nbsp;[C-05](#c-05)<br/>•&nbsp;🔴&nbsp;[F-012](#f-012) — SSTI + eval on user controlled username →&nbsp;[C-01](#c-01) | 🔴 **Critical**<br/>Full Server Compromise · Customer Data Exfiltration | ❶ [M-033](#m-033)<br/>❶ [M-006](#m-006) |
| <a id="path-cross-site-scripting"></a>⑥ | **Output Encoding / Cross-Site Scripting** _(T·I)_<br/>Angular components bypass default template escaping by calling bypassSecurityTrustHtml, and search results render user-controlled input into the DOM without sanitisation. | •&nbsp;🟠&nbsp;[F-018](#f-018) — DOM XSS via search result rendering search →&nbsp;[C-02](#c-02)<br/>•&nbsp;🟠&nbsp;[F-028](#f-028) — JWT stored in localStorage XSS accessible token storage →&nbsp;[C-02](#c-02)<br/>•&nbsp;🟠&nbsp;[F-037](#f-037) — Stored XSS via trust HTML bypass in admin panel →&nbsp;[C-02](#c-02) | 🟠 **High**<br/>Customer Session Hijack | ❷ [M-022](#m-022)<br/>❷ [M-020](#m-020) |
| <a id="path-cross-site-request-forgery"></a>⑦ | **CSRF / Permissive CORS** _(S·T)_<br/>State-changing endpoints accept requests without CSRF tokens or SameSite cookie constraints; combined with wildcard CORS, cross-origin requests from attacker-controlled pages reach authenticated API routes. | •&nbsp;🟠&nbsp;[F-015](#f-015) — No CSRF protection on state changing endpoints →&nbsp;[C-01](#c-01)<br/>•&nbsp;🟠&nbsp;[F-016](#f-016) — Wildcard CORS allows all origins on sensitive endpoints →&nbsp;[C-01](#c-01) | 🟠 **High**<br/>Customer Session Hijack · Customer Data Exfiltration | ❷ [M-009](#m-009)<br/>❷ [M-007](#m-007) |

_STRIDE: S spoofing · T tampering · R repudiation · I information disclosure · D denial of service · E elevation of privilege. Risk, findings, components, impact and Fix are derived deterministically; only the one-line weakness description is authored._

**Verified attack chains.** 2 fully viable ([AC-T-003](#ac-t-003), [AC-T-005](#ac-t-005)); 3 partially blocked ([AC-T-001](#ac-t-001), [AC-T-002](#ac-t-002), [AC-T-006](#ac-t-006)). These chains combine individual findings into end-to-end exploitation paths verified step-by-step against the code - see [§9 Abuse Cases](#9-abuse-cases) for the per-step breakdown and blocking mitigations.

| **4** | [C-01](#c-01) — Express REST API Backend | ❶ [M-003](#m-003) — Hardcoded RSA private key enables JWT forgery lib/insecurity.ts | 🔴 [F-002](#f-002) — Hardcoded RSA private key enables JWT forgery (lib/insecurity.ts) | Medium |
| **5** | [C-01](#c-01) — Express REST API Backend | ❶ [M-004](#m-004) — JWT algorithm none bypass via express jwt lib/insecurity.ts | 🔴 [F-003](#f-003) — JWT algorithm none bypass via express jwt lib/insecurity.ts (package.json) | Medium |
| **6** | [C-01](#c-01) — Express REST API Backend | ❶ [M-006](#m-006) — SSTI + eval on user controlled username routes/userProfile.ts | 🔴 [F-012](#f-012) — SSTI + eval on user controlled username (routes/userProfile.ts) | Medium |
| **7** | [C-01](#c-01) — Express REST API Backend | ❶ [M-012](#m-012) — Confidential files exposed via /ftp directory listing server.ts | 🔴 [F-006](#f-006) — Confidential files exposed via /ftp directory listing (server.ts) | Medium |
| **8** | [C-03](#c-03) — Data Layer (SQLite + MarsDB) | ❶ [M-027](#m-027) — Hardcoded admin credentials in static user data data/static/users.yml | 🔴 [F-004](#f-004) — Hardcoded admin credentials in static user data (data/static/users.yml) | Medium |
| **9** | [C-04](#c-04) — File Upload Service | ❶ [M-029](#m-029) — XXE via XML upload with external entity resolution routes/fileUpload.ts | 🔴 [F-010](#f-010) — XXE via XML upload with external entity resolution (routes/fileUpload.ts) | Medium |
| **10** | [C-05](#c-05) — B2B Order API | ❶ [M-033](#m-033) — RCE via vm.runInContext sandbox escape in B2B order routes/b2bOrder.ts | 🔴 [F-011](#f-011) — RCE via vm.runInContext sandbox escape in B2B order (routes/b2bOrder.ts) | Medium |

*24 additional P1/P2 mitigations capped from the leader-board · 3 P3 backlog items in [§10 Mitigation Register](#10-mitigation-register). Sorted by priority (P1 first), then component, then leverage (most findings first), severity (Critical first), and effort (Low first).*

### Operational Strengths

Operational controls rated Adequate or Partial - grouped into broad clusters (full per-control breakdown in [§7](#7-security-architecture)). Clusters demoted to Weak by open Critical/High findings appear in [§7](#7-security-architecture) instead, not here.

| Strength | What's in Place | Effectiveness | Gap | Mitigates |
|----------------------|----------------------|-------------|----------------------|----------------|
| **Container & Supply-Chain Hardening** | _Build-time and runtime hardening - minimal base image, non-root execution, dependency inventory._<br/>Automated SCA scanning<br/>CI/CD security scanning<br/>Container image pinning | ✅ Adequate | - | - |
| **Hardened HTTP Stack** | _Browser-facing HTTP hardening - security headers, cookie flags, cross-origin policy, and abuse-protection limits._<br/>Rate limiting (general) | ⚠️ Partial | Bypassed by 3 High finding(s) of the kind this cluster is supposed to prevent - e.g. [F-015](#t-015), [F-016](#t-016), [F-026](#t-026). | - |


**Bottom line:** These controls narrow specific attack surfaces but none eliminates a Critical finding on its own.

    L_T009["T-009 - Unsalted MD5 hashes in database…"]:::leaf
    L_T010["T-010 - XXE via XML upload"]:::leaf

## 1. System Overview

**Repository:** https://github.com/juice-shop/juice-shop

### Scope

This threat model covers 5 components of juice-shop: **Express REST API Backend**, **Angular Single-Page Application**, **Data Layer (SQLite + MarsDB)**, **File Upload Service**, **B2B Order API**.

**Out of scope:** third-party hosted dependencies, browser runtime, operating-system kernel, and the underlying network infrastructure.

---

## 3. Attack Walkthroughs

This section walks through how the highest-risk findings are exploited - one short walkthrough per Critical, each with attack steps, a focused sequence diagram, and the primary mitigation. The cross-finding view (which weaknesses combine toward the worst-case goal, and where one fix severs several paths) is in the [Critical Attack Tree](#critical-attack-tree). Full per-finding context - severity rationale, assets, detection signals - is in the [§8 Findings Register](#8-findings-register) row for each finding.

### 3.1 SQL injection in login routes/login.ts

**Source:** [F-001](#f-001) — `routes/login.ts:37`

Severity **Critical** ([CWE-89](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/89.html)). STRIDE: Spoofing. See [§8 T-001](#t-001) for the full register row.

**Attack Steps**

1. The login handler constructs a raw SQL query using string interpolation of user-supplied email and password hash values.
2. An attacker can use SQL injection payloads (e.g.
3. `' OR '1'='1'--`) in the email field to bypass authentication entirely and gain access as any user, including admin.

**Sequence Diagram**

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    actor Attacker
    participant API
    participant DB
    Note over API: backend-api - routes/login.ts:37
    Note over DB: Database
    Attacker->>API: Crafted HTTP request to the affected endpoint with classical OR 1=1 payload
    API->>DB: SQL built by string interpolation - payload becomes query
    DB-->>API: First matching row regardless of intended predicate
    API-->>Attacker: 200 OK with authenticated session / leaked rows
    alt Current state — T-001
        Attacker->>API: Crafted request exploiting T-001 succeeds
        API-->>Attacker: Exploitation confirmed
    else After M-001 — SQL injection in login routes/login.ts
        Attacker->>API: Same request after the fix is applied
        API-->>Attacker: Request rejected
    end
```

**Key takeaway:** Until M-001 (SQL injection in login `routes/login.ts`) lands, T-001 is exploitable at `routes/login.ts:37` (Critical-severity, [CWE-89](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/89.html)).

**Defense in Depth**

- Primary mitigation: [M-001](#m-001) (SQL injection in login `routes/login.ts`)

### 3.2 Hardcoded RSA private key enables JWT forgery lib/insecurit…

**Source:** [F-002](#f-002) — `lib/insecurity.ts:26`

Severity **Critical** ([CWE-321](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/321.html)). STRIDE: Spoofing. See [§8 T-002](#t-002) for the full register row.

**Attack Steps**

1. The RSA private key used to sign all JWT authentication tokens is hardcoded as a string literal in `lib/insecurity.ts`.
2. Anyone with access to the source code (public GitHub repository) can use this key to sign JWT tokens as any user, including admin, and authenticate without a valid password.
3. Clone the public repository and locate the cryptographic key at `lib/insecurity.ts:26`.

**Sequence Diagram**

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    actor Attacker
    participant Repo
    participant App
    Note over Repo: Public repository
    Note over App: backend-api - lib/insecurity.ts:26
    Attacker->>Repo: Clone or browse - extract private key from lib/insecurity.ts line 26
    Attacker->>Attacker: Sign forged token / signature with the extracted key
    Attacker->>App: Submit forged artefact in normal authentication flow
    App-->>Attacker: Request accepted as authentic - signature validates
    alt Current state — T-002
        Attacker->>Repo: Crafted request exploiting T-002 succeeds
        Repo-->>Attacker: Exploitation confirmed
    else After M-003 — Hardcoded RSA private key enables JWT forgery lib/insecurity
        Attacker->>Repo: Same request after the fix is applied
        Repo-->>Attacker: Request rejected
    end
```

**Key takeaway:** Until M-003 (Hardcoded RSA private key enables JWT forgery lib/insecurity) lands, T-002 is exploitable at `lib/insecurity.ts:26` (Critical-severity, [CWE-321](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/321.html)).

**Defense in Depth**

- Primary mitigation: [M-003](#m-003) (Hardcoded RSA private key enables JWT forgery `lib/insecurity.ts`)

### 3.3 JWT algorithm none bypass via express jwt lib/insecurity.ts

**Source:** [F-003](#f-003) — `package.json:1`

Severity **Critical** ([CWE-327](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/327.html)). STRIDE: Spoofing. See [§8 T-003](#t-003) for the full register row.

**Attack Steps**

1. express-jwt version 0.1.3 is vulnerable to JWT algorithm confusion attacks (`CVE-2015-9235` class).
2. An attacker can craft a JWT with `alg: none` and an empty signature, which will be accepted by `express-jwt@0.1.3` without signature verification.
3. This bypasses authentication entirely.

**Sequence Diagram**

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    actor Attacker
    participant App
    Note over App: backend-api - package.json:1
    Attacker->>App: GET /jwks or read RSA public key from the repo
    App-->>Attacker: Public RSA key (intended for RS256 verification)
    Attacker->>Attacker: Sign forged JWT with HS256 using the RSA public key as the HMAC secret (or set alg=none and drop the signature)
    Attacker->>App: Authenticated request carrying the forged token
    App-->>Attacker: 200 OK - server accepts the forged token because it does not enforce algorithm binding
    alt Current state — T-003
        Attacker->>App: Crafted request exploiting T-003 succeeds
        App-->>Attacker: Exploitation confirmed
    else After M-004 — JWT algorithm none bypass via express jwt lib/insecurity.ts
        Attacker->>App: Same request after the fix is applied
        App-->>Attacker: Request rejected
    end
```

**Key takeaway:** Until M-004 (JWT algorithm none bypass via express jwt `lib/insecurity.ts`) lands, T-003 is exploitable at `package.json:1` (Critical-severity, [CWE-327](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/327.html)).

**Defense in Depth**

- Primary mitigation: [M-004](#m-004) (JWT algorithm none bypass via express jwt `lib/insecurity.ts`)

### 3.4 Hardcoded admin credentials in static user data data/static…

**Source:** [F-004](#f-004) — `data/static/users.yml:3`

Severity **Critical** ([CWE-259](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/259.html)). STRIDE: Spoofing. See [§8 T-004](#t-004) for the full register row.

**Attack Steps**

1. The application seeds the database with hardcoded user credentials from `data/static/users.yml`.
2. This includes admin@juice-`sh.op`:admin123, support admin credentials, and multiple customer accounts with passwords.
3. These credentials are in the public GitHub repository and represent permanently compromised accounts.

**Sequence Diagram**

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    actor Attacker
    participant App
    Note over App: data-persistence - data/static/users.yml:3
    Attacker->>App: Crafted request exercising the weakness at data/static/users.yml line 3
    App->>App: Vulnerable branch executes without the missing control
    App-->>Attacker: Response confirms the weakness - CWE-259
    alt Current state — T-004
        Attacker->>App: Crafted request exploiting T-004 succeeds
        App-->>Attacker: Exploitation confirmed
    else After M-027 — Hardcoded admin credentials in static user data data/static/
        Attacker->>App: Same request after the fix is applied
        App-->>Attacker: Request rejected
    end
```

**Key takeaway:** Until M-027 (Hardcoded admin credentials in static user data data/static/) lands, T-004 is exploitable at `data/static/users.yml:3` (Critical-severity, [CWE-259](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/259.html)).

**Defense in Depth**

- Primary mitigation: [M-027](#m-027) (Hardcoded admin credentials in static user data `data/static/users.yml`)

### 3.5 SQL injection in product search routes/search.ts

**Source:** [F-005](#f-005) — `routes/search.ts:23`

Severity **Critical** ([CWE-89](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/89.html)). STRIDE: Information Disclosure. See [§8 T-005](#t-005) for the full register row.

**Attack Steps**

1. The search endpoint constructs a raw SQL LIKE query with unescaped user input.
2. An attacker can use UNION-based SQL injection to extract the full database schema and all user data from the SQLite database without authentication.
3. Identify the vulnerable input parameter - `backend-api` interpolates it directly into a SQL string at `routes/search.ts:23`.

**Sequence Diagram**

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    actor Attacker
    participant API
    participant DB
    Note over API: backend-api - routes/search.ts:23
    Note over DB: Database
    Attacker->>API: Crafted search query (HTML payload in `q=` parameter) with classical OR 1=1 payload
    API->>DB: SQL built by string interpolation - payload becomes query
    DB-->>API: First matching row regardless of intended predicate
    API-->>Attacker: 200 OK with authenticated session / leaked rows
    alt Current state — T-005
        Attacker->>API: Crafted request exploiting T-005 succeeds
        API-->>Attacker: Exploitation confirmed
    else After M-002 — SQL injection in product search routes/search.ts
        Attacker->>API: Same request after the fix is applied
        API-->>Attacker: Request rejected
    end
```

**Key takeaway:** Until M-002 (SQL injection in product search `routes/search.ts`) lands, T-005 is exploitable at `routes/search.ts:23` (Critical-severity, [CWE-89](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/89.html)).

**Defense in Depth**

- Primary mitigation: [M-002](#m-002) (SQL injection in product search `routes/search.ts`)

### 3.6 Confidential files exposed via /ftp directory listing serve…

**Source:** [F-006](#f-006) — `server.ts:270`

Severity **Critical** ([CWE-548](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/548.html)). STRIDE: Information Disclosure. See [§8 T-006](#t-006) for the full register row.

**Attack Steps**

1. The `/ftp` directory is served with full directory listing enabled.
2. It contains confidential business documents: `acquisitions.md` (M&A plans), incident-`support.kdbx` (KeePass password database), `coupons_2013.md.bak`, and `package-lock.json.bak`.
3. These files are publicly accessible without authentication.

**Sequence Diagram**

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    actor Attacker
    participant App
    Note over App: backend-api - server.ts:270
    Attacker->>App: Crafted request exercising the weakness at server.ts line 270
    App->>App: Vulnerable branch executes without the missing control
    App-->>Attacker: Response confirms the weakness - CWE-548
    alt Current state — T-006
        Attacker->>App: Crafted request exploiting T-006 succeeds
        App-->>Attacker: Exploitation confirmed
    else After M-012 — Confidential files exposed via /ftp directory listing server
        Attacker->>App: Same request after the fix is applied
        App-->>Attacker: Request rejected
    end
```

**Key takeaway:** Until M-012 (Confidential files exposed via `/ftp` directory listing server) lands, T-006 is exploitable at `server.ts:270` (Critical-severity, [CWE-548](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/548.html)).

**Defense in Depth**

- Primary mitigation: [M-012](#m-012) (Confidential files exposed via `/ftp` directory listing `server.ts`)

### 3.7 MD5 password hashing trivially reversible lib/insecurity.ts

**Source:** [F-007](#f-007) — `lib/insecurity.ts:43`

Severity **Critical** ([CWE-916](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/916.html)). STRIDE: Information Disclosure. See [§8 T-007](#t-007) for the full register row.

**Attack Steps**

1. All user passwords are hashed with unsalted `MD5`.
2. `MD5` is cryptographically broken and password databases can be cracked entirely using rainbow tables within minutes.
3. Any attacker who obtains a copy of the database has immediate access to all plaintext passwords.

**Sequence Diagram**

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    actor Attacker
    participant App
    Note over App: backend-api - lib/insecurity.ts:43
    Attacker->>Attacker: Craft JWT with header alg=none and admin claims
    Attacker->>App: Crafted HTTP request to the affected endpoint with the unsigned token in Authorization
    App->>App: Validate token without pinning the signing algorithm
    App-->>Attacker: 200 OK - request authorised under forged admin identity
    alt Current state — T-007
        Attacker->>App: Crafted request exploiting T-007 succeeds
        App-->>Attacker: Exploitation confirmed
    else After M-005 — MD5 password hashing trivially reversible lib/insecurity.ts
        Attacker->>App: Same request after the fix is applied
        App-->>Attacker: Request rejected
    end
```

**Key takeaway:** Until M-005 (`MD5` password hashing trivially reversible `lib/insecurity.ts`) lands, T-007 is exploitable at `lib/insecurity.ts:43` (Critical-severity, [CWE-916](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/916.html)).

**Defense in Depth**

- Primary mitigation: [M-005](#m-005) (`MD5` password hashing trivially reversible `lib/insecurity.ts`)

### 3.8 User passwords stored as unsalted MD5 data at rest models/u…

**Source:** [F-009](#f-009) — `models/user.ts:1`

Severity **Critical** ([CWE-916](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/916.html)). STRIDE: Information Disclosure. See [§8 T-009](#t-009) for the full register row.

**Attack Steps**

1. The SQLite database stores user passwords as unsalted `MD5` hashes.
2. If an attacker gains read access to the database file (via SQL injection, path traversal, or server compromise), all password hashes can be cracked using rainbow tables instantly.
3. Common passwords like 'admin123' would be recovered in milliseconds.

**Sequence Diagram**

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    actor Attacker
    participant App
    Note over App: data-persistence - models/user.ts:1
    Attacker->>Attacker: Craft JWT with header alg=none and admin claims
    Attacker->>App: Crafted HTTP request to the affected endpoint with the unsigned token in Authorization
    App->>App: Validate token without pinning the signing algorithm
    App-->>Attacker: 200 OK - request authorised under forged admin identity
    alt Current state — T-009
        Attacker->>App: Crafted request exploiting T-009 succeeds
        App-->>Attacker: Exploitation confirmed
    else After M-005 — MD5 password hashing trivially reversible lib/insecurity.ts
        Attacker->>App: Same request after the fix is applied
        App-->>Attacker: Request rejected
    end
```

**Key takeaway:** Until M-005 (`MD5` password hashing trivially reversible `lib/insecurity.ts`) lands, T-009 is exploitable at `models/user.ts:1` (Critical-severity, [CWE-916](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/916.html)).

**Defense in Depth**

- Primary mitigation: [M-005](#m-005) (`MD5` password hashing trivially reversible `lib/insecurity.ts`)

### 3.9 XXE via XML upload with external entity resolution routes/f…

**Source:** [F-010](#f-010) — `routes/fileUpload.ts:45`

Severity **Critical** ([CWE-611](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/611.html)). STRIDE: Information Disclosure. See [§8 T-010](#t-010) for the full register row.

**Attack Steps**

1. The XML upload handler uses libxmljs2 with `noent: true`, which enables external entity processing.
2. An attacker can upload an XML file with an XXE payload pointing to file:///etc/passwd or internal network URLs, causing the server to disclose local file contents or SSRF to internal services.
3. Send the crafted payload to the endpoint backed by `routes/fileUpload.ts:45`.

**Sequence Diagram**

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    actor Attacker
    participant App
    Note over App: file-upload-service - routes/fileUpload.ts:45
    Attacker->>App: Crafted request exercising the weakness at routes/fileUpload.ts line 45
    App->>App: Vulnerable branch executes without the missing control
    App-->>Attacker: Response confirms the weakness - CWE-611
    alt Current state — T-010
        Attacker->>App: Crafted request exploiting T-010 succeeds
        App-->>Attacker: Exploitation confirmed
    else After M-029 — XXE via XML upload with external entity resolution routes/fi
        Attacker->>App: Same request after the fix is applied
        App-->>Attacker: Request rejected
    end
```

**Key takeaway:** Until M-029 (XXE via XML upload with external entity resolution routes/fi) lands, T-010 is exploitable at `routes/fileUpload.ts:45` (Critical-severity, [CWE-611](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/611.html)).

**Defense in Depth**

- Primary mitigation: [M-029](#m-029) (XXE via XML upload with external entity resolution `routes/fileUpload.ts`)

### 3.10 RCE via vm.runInContext sandbox escape in B2B order routes/…

**Source:** [F-011](#f-011) — `routes/b2bOrder.ts:22`

Severity **Critical** ([CWE-94](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/94.html)). STRIDE: Elevation of Privilege. See [§8 T-011](#t-011) for the full register row.

**Attack Steps**

1. The B2B order endpoint evaluates user-supplied `orderLinesData` using `notevil` sandboxed eval inside `vm.runInContext`.
2. The `notevil` library is not a true security sandbox - it can be escaped via prototype pollution or constructor access.
3. An attacker can achieve full server-side code execution by sending a crafted `orderLinesData` payload.

**Sequence Diagram**

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    actor Attacker
    participant API
    participant Sandbox
    Note over API: b2b-api - routes/b2bOrder.ts:22
    Note over Sandbox: Eval / VM context
    Attacker->>API: Crafted HTTP request to the affected endpoint with JS payload escaping the sandbox
    API->>Sandbox: Pass attacker-controlled code to eval / vm.runInContext
    Sandbox->>Sandbox: Prototype-walk reaches the host realm - RCE
    API-->>Attacker: Command output / arbitrary side effects
    alt Current state — T-011
        Attacker->>API: Crafted request exploiting T-011 succeeds
        API-->>Attacker: Exploitation confirmed
    else After M-033 — RCE via vm.runInContext sandbox escape in B2B order routes/b
        Attacker->>API: Same request after the fix is applied
        API-->>Attacker: Request rejected
    end
```

**Key takeaway:** Until M-033 (RCE via `vm.runInContext` sandbox escape in B2B order routes/b) lands, T-011 is exploitable at `routes/b2bOrder.ts:22` (Critical-severity, [CWE-94](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/94.html)).

**Defense in Depth**

- Primary mitigation: [M-033](#m-033) (RCE via `vm.runInContext` sandbox escape in B2B order `routes/b2bOrder.ts`)

### 3.11 SSTI + eval on user controlled username routes/userProfile.…

**Source:** [F-012](#f-012) — `routes/userProfile.ts:62`

Severity **Critical** ([CWE-94](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/94.html)). STRIDE: Elevation of Privilege. See [§8 T-012](#t-012) for the full register row.

**Attack Steps**

1. The user profile page uses `eval()` on a server-side template that includes the user's username.
2. If a username matches the pattern `#{(.*)}`, the captured group is passed to `eval()` in a Node\.js context.
3. An attacker who can set their username to `#{process.mainModule.require('child_process').execSync('cat /etc/passwd')}` achieves server-side code execution.

**Sequence Diagram**

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    actor Attacker
    participant API
    participant Sandbox
    Note over API: backend-api - routes/userProfile.ts:62
    Note over Sandbox: Eval / VM context
    Attacker->>API: Crafted HTTP request to the affected endpoint with JS payload escaping the sandbox
    API->>Sandbox: Pass attacker-controlled code to eval / vm.runInContext
    Sandbox->>Sandbox: Prototype-walk reaches the host realm - RCE
    API-->>Attacker: Command output / arbitrary side effects
    alt Current state — T-012
        Attacker->>API: Crafted request exploiting T-012 succeeds
        API-->>Attacker: Exploitation confirmed
    else After M-006 — SSTI + eval on user controlled username routes/userProfile.t
        Attacker->>API: Same request after the fix is applied
        API-->>Attacker: Request rejected
    end
```

**Key takeaway:** Until M-006 (SSTI + eval on user controlled username routes/userProfile.t) lands, T-012 is exploitable at `routes/userProfile.ts:62` (Critical-severity, [CWE-94](https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/94.html)).

**Defense in Depth**

- Primary mitigation: [M-006](#m-006) (SSTI + eval on user controlled username `routes/userProfile.ts`)

## 5. Attack Surface

Network-reachable entry points classified by authentication requirement. Each row links to the threat(s) referenced in its **Notes** column. The **Risk** column reflects the highest-severity linked finding.

### 5.1 Unauthenticated Entry Points (16)

| Method | Route | Risk | Notes |
|------|--------------------------|----------|------------------------------------|
| POST | `/file-upload` | 🔴 Critical | 🔴 [F-010](#f-010) (XXE via XML upload with external entity resolution)<br/>🟠 [F-026](#f-026) (SSRF via profile image URL upload)<br/>🟠 [F-031](#f-031) (Denial of Service via XML billion laughs attack)<br/>ZIP directory traversal; XML XXE via libxmljs2 `noent:true`; YAML upload |
| GET | `/ftp (and files)` | 🔴 Critical | 🔴 [F-006](#f-006) (Confidential files exposed via `/ftp` directory listing)<br/>Directory listing exposing confidential files: `acquisitions.md`, incident-`support.kdbx`, coupons |
| POST | `/profile/image/url` | 🔴 Critical | 🟠 [F-026](#f-026) (SSRF via profile image URL upload)<br/>🔴 [F-012](#f-012) (SSTI + eval on user controlled username)<br/>🟠 [F-015](#f-015) (No CSRF protection on state changing endpoints)<br/>SSRF via URL-based profile image upload; fetches arbitrary URLs server-side |
| GET | `/rest/products/search` | 🔴 Critical | 🔴 [F-005](#f-005) (SQL injection in product search)<br/>SQL injection via q parameter; raw Sequelize query with string interpolation |
| GET | `/rest/track-order/:id` | 🔴 Critical | 🔴 [F-011](#f-011) (RCE via `vm.runInContext` sandbox escape in B2B order)<br/>🟠 [F-029](#f-029) (Denial of Service via B2B infinite loop)<br/>🟡 [F-040](#f-040) (No audit trail for B2B orders)<br/>SQL injection via order ID parameter |
| POST | `/rest/user/login` | 🔴 Critical | 🟠 [F-030](#f-030) (No rate limiting on login endpoint)<br/>🔴 [F-001](#f-001) (SQL injection in login)<br/>SQL injection via email/password fields; no rate limiting on this endpoint |
| PUT | `/api/Products/:id` | 🟠 High | 🔴 [F-013](#f-013) (Unauthenticated product modification via PUT `/api/Products/:id`)<br/>🔴 [F-018](#f-018) (DOM XSS via search result rendering search `result.component.ts`)<br/>Authorization intentionally commented out - unauthenticated product modification |
| POST | `/api/Users (registration)` | 🟠 High | 🔴 [F-019](#f-019) (User enumeration via `/api/Users`)<br/>🟠 [F-030](#f-030) (No rate limiting on login endpoint)<br/>User registration; allows setting role field (mass assignment if validation bypassed) |
| GET | `/encryptionkeys/:file` | 🟠 High | 🟠 [F-021](#f-021) (JWT public key and `premium.key` exposed at `/encryptionkeys`)<br/>Exposes JWT public key (`jwt.pub`) and `premium.key`; directory listing enabled |
| GET | `/metrics` | 🟠 High | 🟠 [F-026](#f-026) (SSRF via profile image URL upload)<br/>🟡 [F-041](#f-041) (Prometheus metrics exposed unauthenticated at `/metrics`)<br/>Prometheus metrics endpoint; reveals application internals and request statistics |
| GET | `/rest/products/:id/reviews` | 🟠 High | 🔴 [F-025](#f-025) (NoSQL injection via \$where clause in product reviews `showProductReviews.ts`)<br/>🔴 [F-017](#f-017) (NoSQL injection in review update no author verification `updateProductReviews.ts`)<br/>NoSQL injection via \$where clause in MarsDB query; sleep injection DoS possible |
| GET | `/support/logs/:file` | 🟠 High | 🟠 [F-020](#f-020) (Access logs exposed via `/support/logs` directory listing)<br/>Access log directory listing; reveals user activity and IP addresses |

## 7. Security Architecture

This chapter is organized by security-control category. The architecture section avoids artificial control IDs and finding-ID columns in overview tables. Findings are listed only where the affected control is described.

_[§7](#7-security-architecture) schema v2 (13-section control-category layout). Cataloged controls: 29 total - 1 adequate, 4 partial, 1 weak, 16 unsafe, 7 missing. Linked threats: 38._

**How to read the verdicts.** Every control category (and every sub-control below it) carries exactly one status. The two red verdicts do **not** mean the same thing - this is the distinction that decides what you have to do about a finding:

| Status | Meaning | What it asks of you |
|----------|------------------------------------|------------------------|
| 🟢 Adequate | Control is present and sound | Nothing - keep it |
| 🟡 Partial | Present, but with meaningful gaps | Close the gap |
| 🟠 Weak | Present, but has exploitable gaps | Strengthen it |
| 🔴 Unsafe | **Present and relied upon, but defeated / trivially bypassable** | **Fix the existing control** |
| 🔴 Missing | **Control was never built** | **Add the control** |
| - | Not applicable to this codebase | - |

So "🔴 Unsafe" on a control category does *not* mean the control is absent - it means the control exists but does not hold (e.g. an `MD5` password hash, a raw-SQL query path, a hardcoded signing key). "🔴 Missing" is reserved for controls that were never built (e.g. no Content-Security-Policy header).

### 7.2 Identity and Authentication Controls

**Verdict:** 🔴 Unsafe

<!-- The line below is mechanically derived from the controls table — LLM must not re-author it. -->
**Controls covered:** [7.2.1 Password-Based Authentication](#721-password-based-authentication) · [7.2.2 MFA / TOTP](#722-mfa-totp) · [7.2.3 OAuth / OIDC Federated Login](#723-oauth-oidc-federated-login)

- [7.2.1 Password-Based Authentication](#password-based-authentication)
- [7.2.2 MFA / TOTP](#mfa-totp)
- [7.2.3 OAuth / OIDC Federated Login](#oauth-oidc-federated-login)

**Implemented controls:** JWT-based session tokens issued on every successful login, TOTP-based 2FA available as an optional user setting, OAuth login adapter via Google federated identity.

**Assessment:** Authentication boundaries are defeated at the key-management layer and the credential-verification layer simultaneously. The RSA private key for JWT signing is committed in `lib/insecurity.ts`; the `express-jwt` middleware also accepts `alg:none`, meaning tokens can be forged without the key at all. Password login queries the database through raw SQL string interpolation at `routes/login.ts:37`, which an attacker can short-circuit without a valid credential. Rate limiting on the login endpoint is absent. Each successful flow terminates in the server issuing a session token; the signing, validation, propagation, storage, and lifecycle of that token are described in [§7.3 Session and Token Controls](#73-session-and-token-controls).

<!-- §7.2 AUTH-MECHANISMS-FROZEN — deterministic inventory, pregenerator-owned. DO NOT EDIT. -->
**Authentication mechanisms (at a glance).** Every authentication mechanism detected on the application, its effective status, where it is assessed, and its linked findings. Controls are catalogued by domain, so JWT/session handling is assessed under [§7.3 Session and Token Controls](#73-session-and-token-controls) and password hashing under [§7.9 Cryptography Secrets and Data Protection](#79-cryptography-secrets-and-data-protection).

| Mechanism | Status | Assessed in | Findings |
|----------------------|---------|-----------|------------------------------------------------|
| Password login | 🔴 Missing | [§7.2](#72-identity-and-authentication-controls) | - |
| Password storage (hashing) | 🔴 Unsafe | [§7.9](#79-cryptography-secrets-and-data-protection) | 🔴 [F-007](#f-007) — MD5 password hashing trivially reversible lib/insecurity.ts<br/>🔴 [F-009](#f-009) — User passwords stored as unsalted MD5 data at rest models/user.ts |
| JWT / bearer-token session | 🔴 Unsafe | [§7.3](#73-session-and-token-controls) | 🔴 [F-002](#f-002) — Hardcoded RSA private key enables JWT forgery lib/insecurity.ts<br/>🔴 [F-003](#f-003) — JWT algorithm none bypass via express jwt lib/insecurity.ts<br/>🟠 [F-021](#f-021) — JWT public key and `premium.key` exposed at /encryptionkeys server.ts<br/>🟠 [F-028](#f-028) — JWT stored in localStorage XSS accessible token storage login.component.ts |
| Session-token storage | 🔴 Unsafe | [§7.3](#73-session-and-token-controls) | 🟠 [F-028](#f-028) — JWT stored in localStorage XSS accessible token storage login.component.ts |
